project_overview.project_name: Empowering Microfinance and Enterprises for Resilience and Growth (EMERGe) Project
project_overview.project_development_objective: To support the demand for and supply of finance for micro and small enterprises in Afghanistan, focusing on women's financial inclusion
project_overview.beneficiaries: Private sector microfinance institutions (MFPs), their borrowers (existing and future), micro and small enterprises (MSMEs), with particular emphasis on women-owned and women-led businesses

geographic_scope.fragile_or_conflict_affected_areas: yes - Afghanistan is classified as a fragile, conflict, and violence (FCV) affected state. The project operates in post-conflict context following August 15, 2021 events. Geographic selection based on implementing partners' existing presence and microfinance providers' footprint

key_components.description: Strengthens supply of finance through: (1a) Conditional Capital Support Grants (CSGs) to three private sector MFPs to clean balance sheets of non-performing loans (NPLs) originating before August 2021; (1b) Technical assistance for transition to Islamic Finance, business process digitization, and operational capacity building to MFPs and CAEDO; (1c) Component management and implementation costs
key_components.financing: US$10 million (1a: US$7 million; 1b: US$1.8 million; 1c: US$1.2 million)

key_components.component_name: Component 2: Building a Pipeline of Bankable Micro and Small Enterprises
key_components.description: Supports demand for credit through: (2a) Business development services (BDS) including capacity building for small enterprises (US$750,000) and microenterprises (US$750,000) for credit readiness; (2b) Credit Viability Fund (CVF) capitalization to help MSMEs access formal financial channels through fee/commission subsidies, collateral support, and de-risking for women-led MSMEs; (2c) Component management and implementation costs
key_components.financing: US$6 million (2a: US$1.5 million; 2b: US$3.6 million; 2c: US$900,000)

implementation_arrangements.implementing_agency: Aga Khan Foundation USA (AKF-USA) as grant recipient, with Project Implementation Entities (PIEs): AKF-Afghanistan (Component 2) and Afghan Credit Guarantee Foundation (ACGF) (Component 1). AKF-USA will establish Project Implementation Unit (PIU) in Afghanistan
implementation_arrangements.coordination_mechanisms: Project Implementation Unit (PIU) established by AKF-USA; Subsidiary Agreements (SAs) between AKF-USA and each PIE; coordination with ARTF donors through Economic Development Strategic Thematic Working Group; collaboration with development partners through Afghanistan Consultation Group; quarterly virtual implementation support missions by World Bank; bi-weekly progress monitoring meetings; ARTF Monitoring Agent (MA) for independent monitoring
implementation_arrangements.procurement_approach: World Bank Procurement Regulations for IPF (November 2020); STEP (Systematic Tracking of Exchanges in Procurement) used by both PIEs; prior review thresholds: Works US$10 million, Goods/IT/Non-consulting services US$2 million, Consulting firms US$1 million, Individual consultants US$0.3 million; open international competition for goods/services ≥US$200,000; open national competition for ≤US$200,000; Request for Quotations for ≤US$50,000; World Bank Standard Procurement Documents; Project Procurement Strategy for Development (PPSD) finalized
implementation_arrangements.monitoring_and_evaluation: ARTF Monitoring Agent (MA) conducts independent data collection and M&E; implementing agencies responsible for regular data collection and analysis; quarterly, semi-annual, and annual project monitoring reports; M&E framework presented in Project Operations Manual (POM); baseline and follow-up data collection; annual M&E reviews and lessons learned; M&E visits and surveys among beneficiaries and stakeholders; 6-month frequency for most indicators; external audits annually by June 30; internal audits conducted by AKF-Afghanistan Internal Audit Department (functionally independent) and ACGF (either internal unit or private firm)

identified_risks.risk_description: Interim Taliban Administration (ITA) lacks international recognition, creating significant political uncertainty. Restrictions on women's economic activities and mobility pose high risks to project implementation and beneficiary access, particularly for women beneficiaries. Policy uncertainty regarding women's ability to continue working
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Lead PIEs (AKF-Afghanistan and ACGF) handle contract management, implementation, performance management, monitoring, and evaluation; utilize existing donor coordination mechanisms (Strategic Thematic Working Group for private sector); Entry Criteria for Access (ECA) established: 'no restrictions on the ability of women to attain credit from MFPs' to assess whether conditions permit implementation; continuous engagement with development partners; Credit Viability Fund includes de-risking product for women-led MSMEs to insure loans against policy changes by ITA

identified_risks.risk_description: Heavy reliance on external aid with potential reduction in civilian support will strain fiscal sustainability and service delivery. Constrained banking system exacerbates risks in managing funds and maintaining liquidity. Economy contracted 25% since August 2021 (20.7% in 2021, 6.2% in 2022). Persistent low aggregate demand, deflation, and reduced humanitarian support. Expected continued returns of 1.46 million Afghans from Pakistan and Iran in 2024
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Recapitalization plans for MFPs to improve management of funds and liquidity; movement toward market-based approaches in private sector; support of enterprises to improve sustainability of economic environment and service delivery; project design focuses on private sector-led economic recovery as integral to building resilience and stability

identified_risks.risk_description: Weak governance and changing policies around Shariah-compliance in financial sector. Mandatory transition to Islamic Finance creates operational challenges. Changes in policies relative to female engagement in private sector. MFPs becoming more cautious in lending to women; average loan size to women has decreased to half that of male borrowers
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Continuous engagement with development partners; recapitalization plans aimed at improving governance of MFPs (Component 1); Credit Viability Fund (Component 2) directly addresses risks around women's engagement in private sector as relates to credit; technical assistance to MFPs for transition to Islamic Finance; product development support for women-focused lending

identified_risks.risk_category: Institutional Capacity for Implementation and Sustainability
identified_risks.risk_description: Complex structure of implementing arrangements with multiple PIEs (AKF-Afghanistan and ACGF) and short 15-month implementation timeline create significant risks. Capacity constraints in fragile state context. Departure of technical staff from Da Afghanistan Bank (DAB). Limited institutional capacity in post-conflict environment
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Detailed Subsidiary Agreements (SAs) and Project Operations Manual (POM) developed to ensure risks are mitigated; AKF-USA has experience implementing WB projects (WERP); ACGF previously implemented WB projects (SAFI); PIU established with qualified staff meeting WB-agreed qualifications; World Bank provides frequent implementation support and technical assistance; ARTF MA provides independent monitoring; flexibility built into project design for mid-course corrections

identified_risks.risk_description: Inherent fiduciary risk assessed as High. Key risks include: potential implementation delays due to complex operating environment; limited knowledge and experience of implementing partners with World Bank Procurement Regulations; supply chain constraints and delays; lack of contract management capacity; potential restrictions on local bank operations; AML/CFT risks in fund transfers; complex fund flow arrangements in restrictive environment
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Residual fiduciary risk reduced to Substantial through mitigation measures: Financial Management and Procurement Manual to be developed within 45 days of effectiveness; detailed staffing and operating cost plan with quarterly/yearly breakdowns; ARTF MA monitoring of compliance; AKF-Afghanistan to leverage procurement expertise from WERP; ACGF to hire qualified national procurement employee(s) familiar with WB procedures; World Bank pre-review of complex and high-value contracts; quarterly reporting and periodic review of procurement progress; STEP used for procurement tracking; detailed POM with control framework; external audits annually; internal audits conducted; AML/CFT measures detailed in POM and SAs; anti-corruption declarations required from PIU/PIE staff; confidentiality agreements for evaluation committees

identified_risks.risk_description: Complex operating environment creates substantial stakeholder risks. Uncertainty regarding international community's engagement with ITA. Multiple stakeholders with varying interests (MFPs, enterprises, donors, government). Potential resistance or lack of cooperation from private sector actors
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Knowledge exchange platforms; internal World Bank-IFC collaboration; consultations with private sector and civil society players for timely feedback; ARTF MA reports provide timely information by soliciting views of direct and indirect project beneficiaries; Stakeholder Engagement Plan (SEP) to be prepared and disclosed; grievance redress mechanisms in place with both AKF and ACGF

identified_risks.risk_description: Ensuring safe and unimpeded access to all areas, including beneficiaries, particularly women, poses additional high risks. Post-conflict environment with ongoing security concerns. Restrictions on women's mobility and ability to travel
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Project design leverages existing networks of implementing partners (AKF-Afghanistan and ACGF) with established presence in target provinces; use of community-based savings groups (CBSGs) for microenterprise identification; existing pipeline of 543 businesses for small enterprise program; flexibility in implementation arrangements to adapt to security situation

identified_risks.risk_description: Moderate environmental and social risks. Potential risks related to inequality, discrimination, and gender vulnerability in access to finance. Risks of sexual exploitation, abuse, and harassment. Potential environmental impacts from small and micro-enterprise activities (waste management, resource management, pollution, occupational health and safety)
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Environmental and Social Management Framework (ESMF) to be prepared, consulted, disclosed, and adopted by AKF prior to project commencement; ACGF has established Environmental and Social Management System (ESMS) in line with ESS9 requirements; Environmental and Social Commitment Plan (ESCP) covering both components; E&S screening and mitigation procedures for Component 2 activities; Exclusion Lists/Negative Lists to be implemented by ACGF and AKF with staff training; sub-sector specific guidelines and manuals for MFP loan officers; sexual exploitation and abuse/sexual harassment action plan; grievance mechanisms in place for both AKF and ACGF; E&S provisions in Conditional CSG Agreements and CVF Agreements

triggered_safeguard_policies: ESS 1: Assessment and Management of Environmental and Social Risks and Impacts
ESS 3: Resource Efficiency and Pollution Prevention and Management
ESS 10: Stakeholder Engagement and Information Disclosure
safeguards_and_social.grievance_redress_mechanism: yes - Both AKF and ACGF have existing grievance mechanisms in place for project stakeholders to lodge project-related complaints. Project includes grievance redress indicator: 'Recorded grievances that are resolved within 4 weeks (reported for men and women)' with target of 80% resolution rate. World Bank's Grievance Redress Service (GRS) available at http://www.worldbank.org/GRS. World Bank's Accountability Mechanism (AM) including Inspection Panel and Dispute Resolution Service available at https://accountability.worldbank.org
safeguards_and_social.gender_considerations: Strong gender focus throughout project. Project Development Objective explicitly focuses on women's financial inclusion. Gender gap in financial inclusion is substantial: only 5% of Afghan women own financial transaction account vs. 10% of men; financial inclusion for women declining from 7% (2017) to 5% (2021). Women comprise 42% of MFP borrowers but have suffered disproportionately in transition to Islamic finance with average loan size decreased to half that of male borrowers. Project targets: 40% of loans disbursed to women (up from 37% baseline); 50% of 800 enterprises made credit-ready to be women-owned; 35% of 500 SMEs receiving training to be women-led; 80% of 3,000 individuals trained in financial literacy to be women; 60% of 500 microentrepreneurs trained to be women; 50% of 700 MSMEs accessing CVF to be women. Technical assistance to MFPs includes support for lending to women and women-led businesses through product development and staff training. Component 2 provides incubation-level TA to women-led small enterprises and supports women-led microenterprises transition from informal savings groups to formal credit. CVF includes de-risking product for women-led MSMEs to insure loans against policy changes by ITA. Entry Criteria for Access (ECA): 'no restrictions on the ability of women to attain credit from MFPs'
vulnerable_groups: Returnees and refugees (1.46 million expected returns from Pakistan and Iran in 2024)
Food-insecure populations (15.3 million acutely food-insecure; 6 million on brink of starvation)

fcv_relevant_details.conflict_sensitivity: Project explicitly designed for FCV context. Processed under Condensed Procedures for urgent situations and capacity constraints caused by conflict, fragility, and external shocks. Aligns with World Bank Strategy for Fragility, Conflict, and Violence (FCV) 2020-2025 and WB Strategy Update 2024. Project addresses gender gaps in access to finance and aims to enhance economic agency and job opportunities for women. Entry Criteria for Access (ECA) established to ensure women's participation and equity principles are maintained: 'no restrictions on the ability of women to attain credit from MFPs' provides platform for ARTF donors, PIEs, and WB to assess whether conditions permit implementation. Credit Viability Fund includes de-risking product specifically designed to insure women-led MSME loans against policy changes by ITA that may impact their ability to service loans. Project incorporates lessons from previous WB interventions in Afghanistan emphasizing need for sequenced but integrated responses in FCV settings, promoting simplicity and selectivity in design, and allowing for mid-course corrections. Project transitions from emergency support (direct cash/in-kind transfers and basic services) to support for recovery of MFPs to promote access to finance
fcv_relevant_details.fragility_context: Afghanistan classified as fragile state. Key fragility factors: (1) Political uncertainty: ITA lacks international recognition; harsh restrictions on women and girls; policy uncertainty regarding women's economic activities; (2) Economic fragility: 25% economic contraction since August 2021; heavy reliance on external aid with expected declines; stagnant or shrinking economy; persistent low aggregate demand; (3) Humanitarian crisis: 15.3 million acutely food-insecure; 6 million on brink of starvation; (4) Financial sector collapse: formal financial sector shrunk further since August 2021; 12 banks facing solvency risks, shrinking assets, inability to perform core functions; mandatory transition to Islamic Finance; loss of confidence; freezing of offshore assets; withdrawal limits; breakdown of correspondent banking; departure of technical staff from central bank; (5) Structural issues: 90% of adults financially excluded; credit to GDP ratio only 6% (down from 3.1% in 2021); microfinance sector severely impacted with 68% of outstanding loans originating before August 2021 and 90% of these past due >180 days; (6) Labor market disruption: significant decline in employment opportunities and incomes; limited labor demand; (7) Informality: 80% of private sector businesses operate informally; only 4% of women-led businesses formally registered; (8) Climate vulnerability: Afghanistan highly vulnerable to droughts, floods, landslides; women disproportionately affected by climate change; (9) Displacement: over 500,000 Afghans returned from Pakistan since September 2023; up to 1.46 million expected returns in 2024
fcv_relevant_details.security_considerations: High security risks identified. Ensuring safe and unimpeded access to all areas, including beneficiaries, particularly women, poses high risks. Post-conflict environment with ongoing security concerns. Restrictions on women's mobility and ability to travel limit their economic participation. Project mitigates through: leveraging existing networks of implementing partners with established presence in target provinces; using community-based structures (CBSGs) for beneficiary identification; flexibility in implementation to adapt to security situation; focus on home-based services and handicrafts for women (80% of women's loans); geographic focus on 15 provinces where implementing partners have existing presence
fcv_relevant_details.displacement_or_refugees: yes - Significant displacement and refugee flows. Over 500,000 Afghans returned from Pakistan since September 15, 2023. Current projections indicate up to 1.46 million Afghans from Pakistan and Iran may return in 2024. Project design allows nationwide at-scale approach to respond to crises including increased repatriation of Afghan returnees. Project supports livelihoods and income-generating opportunities which are critical for returnee populations. Microfinance and small enterprise support can facilitate economic integration of returnees
